narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 46.3% chance that you would not pass. This probability crossed our threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with the strongest projected outcomes to maintain high bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your LSAT score, Year 3 grade decile, and Year 1 grade decile standing out as the most influential. Among these, your LSAT score of 36—while solid, a bit below the 37.27 average for those who passed—played the biggest role in pulling the prediction toward success, essentially acting as your strongest asset in the eyes of the model. However, your Year 3 decile of 2, much lower than the 5.91 average for bar passers, strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the 6.04 average for successful students, added further weight toward the failure risk, highlighting early academic challenges as a concern. Other elements, like your middle family income bracket—which aligns with about a third of bar passers—and your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 versus their 3.24 average, contributed less directly but didn't offset the top issues. As a full-time student, you're in line with over 90% of those who pass, which is a positive but not decisive factor here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by lagging in those critical grade deciles, even with a competitive LSAT and typical background for success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above 37, strengthening your early and later undergrad deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas, and admissions teams value demonstrated improvement. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, you can shift the odds in your favor.
